fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {D} be a deductive system and let A be an object of \mathcal {D}. A downshift structure for A is defined to be a positive object \mathord { \Downarrow } A and a thunkable map \mathsf { wrap } _{ A } : A \vdash \mathord { \Downarrow } A for which there exists a necessarily unique and linear inverse \mathsf { unwrap } _{ A } : \mathord { \Downarrow } A \vdash A.

Let \mathcal {D} be a deductive system and let A be an object of \mathcal {D}. An upshift structure for A is defined to be a negative object \mathord { \Uparrow } A and a linear map \mathsf { force } _{ A } : \mathord { \Uparrow } A \vdash A for which there exists a necessarily unique and thunkable inverse \mathsf { delay } _{ A } : A \vdash \mathord { \Uparrow } A.

Let \mathcal {D} be a deductive system and let A be an object of \mathcal {D}. A downshift structure for A is defined to be a positive object \mathord { \Downarrow } A and a thunkable map \mathsf { wrap } _{ A } : A \vdash \mathord { \Downarrow } A for which there exists a necessarily unique and linear inverse \mathsf { unwrap } _{ A } : \mathord { \Downarrow } A \vdash A.
Let \mathcal {D} be a deductive system, and let f : A \vdash B be a morphism in \mathcal {D}. Then both the type of (linear, thunkable) inverses of f is a proposition.
Let f:C \vdash D be a morphism in a deductive system \mathcal {D}.

fr:ol.fr:li: We say that f is linear when for all g:B \vdash C and h:A \vdash B, we have h; { \mathopen {} \left ( g;f \right ) \mathclose {}} = { \mathopen {} \left ( h;g \right ) \mathclose {}} ;f.
We say that f is thunkable when for all g:D \vdash E and h : E \vdash F, we have f; { \mathopen {} \left ( g;h \right ) \mathclose {}} = { \mathopen {} \left ( f;g \right ) \mathclose {}} ;h.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: An object B in a deductive system \mathcal {D} is called positive when every map B \vdash C is linear; it is called negative when every map A \vdash B is thunkable.

A deductive system \mathcal {D} is given by a type \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} } of “objects”, and for every pair A,B \in \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} } a set \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}} of morphisms, together with cut and identity laws:

fr:ol.fr:li: cut: for each A,B,C \in \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} } and f \in \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}} and g \in \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( B , C \right ) \mathclose {}}, a morphism f;g \in \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , C \right ) \mathclose {}}.

identity: for each A \in \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} }, a morphism 1 _{ A } \in \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , A \right ) \mathclose {}} such that 1 _{ A } ;f = f and f;1 _{ B } for each f \in \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Equivalently, we can define a duploid to be a univalent preduploid such that every object is merely equipped with both an upshift and a downshift.

To see that the main definition implies our alternative definition, let A be an object of the preduploid; because we are proving that A is merely equipped with both an upshift and a downshift, we may proceed by cases on the polarization of A.

fr:ol.fr:li: If A is positive, we choose the identity map A \vdash A for the downshift and we use our assumption to deduce that A has an upshift.
If A is negative, we choose the identity map A \vdash A for the upshift and we use our assumption to deduce that A has a downshift.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Conversely, assume that every object is merely equipped with both an upshift and a downshift. That a positive object P has an upshift is a proposition, so we can use our assumption that P merely has an upshift. That a negative object N has a downshift is likewise a proposition, so we can use our assumption that N merely has a downshift.

Let N \in \mathcal {D} be a negative object in a positively univalent deductive system; then the type of downshift structures on N is a proposition. In this case, we may refer to the property of N having a downshift.

Let w_0:N \vdash P_0 and w_1:N \vdash P_1 be two downshift structures on N, whose inverse unwrapping maps are written u_0,u_1 respectively. We wish to identity { \mathopen {} \left ( P_0,w_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} with { \mathopen {} \left ( P_1,w_1 \right ) \mathclose {}}.

All four maps w_0,w_1,u_0,u_1 are thunkable: the wrapping maps are thunkable by definition, and the unwrapping maps are thunkable because they are valued in a negative object. Thus we have a thunkably invertible thunkable map u_1;w_0 : P_1 \vdash P_0; because \mathcal {D} is assumed positively univalent, we may identify the pairs { \mathopen {} \left ( P_0, 1 _{ P_0 } :P_0 \vdash P_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} and { \mathopen {} \left ( P_1, { \mathopen {} \left ( u_1;w_0:P_1 \vdash P_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}}. Taking inverses and precomposing with w_0 on both sides, we have { \mathopen {} \left ( P_0,w_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} = { \mathopen {} \left ( P_1,w_1 \right ) \mathclose {}} using the fact that w_0 is thunkable and inverse to u_0.
